content: Inside every cell, there is a molecule called mRNA that acts like a messenger carrying instructions from DNA to the cell’s protein-making machinery. mRNA is made by copying a specific section of DNA that contains the instructions for making a particular protein. The reason cells use mRNA instead of DNA directly is because DNA stays safely inside the nucleus, while mRNA can travel to the part of the cell where proteins are made. Once mRNA reaches the protein-making area, called the ribosome, it is “read” like a coded message to assemble the correct building blocks into a protein. The mRNA sequence is divided into sets of three letters, called codons, each of which corresponds to a specific amino acid, the building blocks of proteins. Scientists have confirmed this code works universally in almost all living things through experiments that matched codons to amino acids and observed how changes altered proteins. This process happens quickly and constantly, allowing cells to produce the proteins they need. Understanding mRNA’s role has led to technologies, like mRNA vaccines, that use this natural system to help our cells fight diseases.
